text: The surgeon performs laparoscopic fundoplication, applying medial traction to the fundus while using a harmonic device to separate the omentum and clear the short gastric vessels. The hiatus hernia is visible in the left upper corner. The left crus and peritoneum are being separated, with the spleen clearly visible. The pars flaccida is dissected, revealing the caudate lobe of the liver. A fan retractor is used to expose the right crus after Nathassan's retractor failed.

The surgeon continues dissection around the right crus, using the harmonic device in the right hand for better control. The peritoneum over the right crus is carefully dissected, revealing the embedded anterior vagus nerve in the esophagus. The fundus is manipulated with anterolateral and anteromedial traction. The hernia sac is visible, entering the posterior mediastinum when the stomach is released. Posterior mobilization of the esophagus is performed, creating a window while ensuring the vagus nerve remains close to the esophagus.

A window is created in the surgical field, allowing the grasper to be maneuvered from the right to the left side without entering the mediastinum or damaging the left crust. The grasper appears on the right side, facilitating further mobilization of the posterior mediastinum and posterior mobilization of the esophagus. A clear space is visible through which the stomach can be brought out for fundoplication.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the fundus of the stomach from the left to the right side, ensuring a floppy fundus for a proper shoe shine effect during the Nissen fundoplication. The anterior and posterior buttocks of the stomach are visible, providing traction over the esophagus. Crural approximation is performed, with sutures placed to maintain a 5-8 mm space between the esophagus and the suture line.

The surgeon begins suturing for crural approximation using a surgeon's knot with athi bond number one non-absorbable suture. The needle is carefully handled to avoid piercing tissue, and the suture is tightened without using the needle. A tumble square knot may be used if there is significant tension in the muscle due to a large hiatus hernia.

The surgeon performs the final step of the procedure by suturing the stomach to itself, creating a wrap. The initial suture involves only the stomach, avoiding the esophagus. Subsequent sutures sandwich the esophagus between two layers of the stomach, ensuring only the muscular layer of the esophagus is pierced to prevent perforation.

The surgeon ties a knot using the two-one-one technique, starting with a double wrap followed by two alternating wraps. The muscular layer of the esophagus is carefully included in the knot, ensuring it is sandwiched between two layers of the stomach without penetrating deeper tissue.

The surgeon applies the second and third knots to secure the wrap posteriorly, creating a floppy fundus with a three-centimeter length. The small suture is tied using precise, small-scale movements to ensure a secure knot.

The surgeon carefully removes the curved needle with a small suture under direct vision, holding the suture tail and slowly withdrawing the cannula. The needle holder is used to take the suture tail out, followed by the needle itself.
